nativeRange.habitat: Lowland habitats including rice paddies, irrigation canals, river backwaters, natural ponds, and slow-moving streams; occasionally found in upland hill streams.

decorPreferences: Dense vegetation for cover and line-of-sight breaks
Tight-fitting lid essential (labyrinth fish; escape risk)

diet.notes: Accepts a variety of foods including quality flake, pellets, and frozen or live foods such as bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, and mosquito larvae. A high-protein diet tends to support best coloration and condition.

goodWith: Fast-swimming barbs (e.g. Tiger Barb, Rosy Barb)
avoidWith: Other male Paradise Fish (severe conspecific aggression)
Small nano fish (e.g. Neon Tetras — risk of predation)

summary: One of the earliest ornamental fish kept in Europe (1860s), the Paradise Fish is a coldwater-tolerant labyrinth fish from East Asia, valued for its vivid tiger-stripe coloration and undemanding water requirements. Males tend to be aggressive and are best kept singly.
careNotes: The Paradise Fish is considered one of the first ornamental fish imported to Europe, introduced around 1869. Males tend to be highly aggressive toward one another and toward other anabantids; keeping two males together in anything but a very large, heavily planted tank is generally inadvisable. Males have also been known to harass and injure females outside of spawning periods — a tank with dense vegetation or a divider is advisable when housing a pair. The species possesses a labyrinth organ that allows it to breathe atmospheric air directly; a tight-fitting lid is important both to prevent escape and to keep the air above the water surface warm and humid, which matters for proper labyrinth organ development in fry. Males have been known to prey on small fish such as neon tetras, and shrimp are generally at risk. Selectively bred color forms are available, including albino, xanthic (yellow), red, and stripeless varieties; wild-type specimens are uncommon in the trade, as most fish available are farm-bred. Some hobbyists report benefits from allowing a cooler winter rest period.
breedingNotes: The Paradise Fish is a bubble-nest builder, breeding in a manner similar to Betta. Males construct nests at the water surface, often anchored in floating vegetation. Spawning is initiated by a typical osphronemid embrace beneath the nest, after which several hundred eggs are typically produced. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–4 days. Fry require infusoria-grade food initially, progressing to microworms and baby brine shrimp as they grow. Males tend to guard the nest actively but lose interest once fry are free-swimming; at that stage adults are generally removed to prevent predation. Slightly raising the temperature and using softer water has been reported to help induce spawning. Breeding is considered accessible for hobbyists with some labyrinth-fish experience.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Primary cross-verification source. Gave temperature range 10–22°C (lower than other sources); 16–26°C consensus used. Confirmed pH 6.0–8.0, Osphronemidae/Anabantiformes taxonomy, native range, bubble-nest breeding, and aggression profile.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 16–26°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 5–19 dH, max size ~6.7 cm SL, native range China/Taiwan/Vietnam. Used as tiebreaker for temperature floor.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Confirmed omnivore diet, aggression profile, 8–10 year lifespan, 20-gallon minimum tank size. Temperature given as 70–82°F (21–28°C); upper end slightly higher than FishBase, averaged in.

fish.breedingNotes: Males construct bubble nests at the water surface and guard eggs and early fry. Several hundred eggs are typical per spawn. Fry require infusoria-grade food at first, progressing to microworms and baby brine shrimp. Adults are generally removed once fry are free-swimming to prevent predation.
